question.text: Scenario: Mr. Arvind Sharma, aged 42, is a senior manager at an IT company earning Rs. 18 lakh per annum (gross). His wife Mrs. Priya works part-time earning Rs. 6 lakh per annum. Their combined monthly expenses are Rs. 1,20,000. They have two children aged 14 and 10. Arvind's current investments include: Equity Mutual Funds Rs. 35 lakh, PPF Rs. 12 lakh (current balance), Bank Fixed Deposits Rs. 8 lakh, Employee PF balance Rs. 18 lakh, and a savings bank balance of Rs. 3 lakh. He has an outstanding home loan of Rs. 22 lakh with EMI of Rs. 25,000/month (included in expenses above) and no other loans. His employer contributes Rs. 1,800/month to PF and he contributes the same. He wants to retire at age 58 and needs a retirement corpus of Rs. 3 crore. He also needs Rs. 30 lakh for his elder child's education in 4 years and Rs. 25 lakh for the younger child in 8 years. Assume equity returns at 12% CAGR and debt returns at 7% CAGR. Inflation is 6% p.a. Question 3 of 5: The monthly savings ratio of the Sharma family (considering gross salary, PF contributions, and expenses of Rs. 1,20,000) is closest to:

explanation.text: **The Logic:** Monthly income = (18,00,000 + 6,00,000)/12 = Rs. 2,00,000. Monthly expenses = Rs. 1,20,000 (includes EMI). Monthly savings = 2,00,000 - 1,20,000 = Rs. 80,000 (which includes PF of Rs. 3,600). Savings ratio = 80,000/2,00,000 = 40%. **The Trap:** Using only Arvind's salary (Rs. 1,50,000/month) gives a different ratio. Use total family income for family savings ratio. **Cross-Exam Context:** Savings ratio calculation is tested in X-A (Ch 3). **Distributor's Pro-Tip:** A 40% savings ratio is excellent — the Sharmas are in a strong position to meet their goals if savings are properly allocated.
